title: Is a portable heater cheaper than central heating?
description: Plug-in heaters cost 26.11p per kWh against roughly 8p for gas central heating. We show exactly when a portable heater works out cheaper, with full UK sums.
blurb: Electricity costs three times more than gas per unit of heat, yet a portable heater still wins in one specific situation.
lede: Every winter the same argument starts up: is it cheaper to drag a fan heater into the room you are sitting in, or to fire up the gas boiler and heat the whole house? The honest answer annoys both camps. Unit for unit, electricity is more than three times the price of gas at the current cap, so a plug-in heater is an expensive way to make warmth. But central heating warms every room on the circuit whether you are in it or not, and an empty house full of warm rooms is the most expensive option of all. Which one wins depends almost entirely on how much of your home you actually need warm, and for how long. This guide puts real numbers on both sides so you can settle the argument for your own house.
short_answer: Per unit of heat, gas central heating costs around 8p per kWh against 26.11p per kWh for any plug-in electric heater, so central heating is roughly three times cheaper for the same warmth. A portable heater only wins when it replaces the whole system: one person, one small room, a few hours. Heating one room electrically typically costs £1 to £1.50 an evening, against £1.80 to £3 for whole-house gas heating. Run two or more portable heaters, or run one all day, and gas wins comfortably.

sections.h2: The only fair comparison: pence per kWh of heat
sections.html:
Comparing a heater's wattage with a boiler's size tells you nothing useful. The number that settles the question is what each system charges you for one kilowatt hour of heat actually delivered into your rooms.

Every plug-in electric heater, whether it is a £15 fan heater or a £150 oil-filled radiator, turns electricity into heat at effectively 100 per cent efficiency. So each kWh of warmth costs you the full electricity unit rate: 26.11p under the current price cap.

Gas is different. The unit rate is only 7.33p per kWh, but a boiler loses some of that energy up the flue. A modern condensing boiler running well converts about 90 per cent of the gas into useful heat, so a kWh of warmth costs about 8.1p. An older or badly set up boiler at 80 per cent efficiency delivers it at around 9.2p. You can nudge that efficiency up for free by lowering your boiler flow temperature, which keeps a condensing boiler in its most efficient operating range.

Heating methodEfficiencyCost per kWh of useful heat
Modern condensing gas boiler, well set up90%8.1p
Older gas boiler80%9.2p
Air source heat pump (typical)300% (COP 3)8.7p
Any plug-in electric heater100%26.1p

At 26.11p per kWh electricity and 7.33p per kWh gas, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026.

So gas heat is roughly a third of the price of electric heat. The interesting case in that table is the heat pump, which moves heat rather than generating it and lands at gas-like running costs even on full-price electricity. If your boiler is nearing the end of its life, our guide to how heat pumps work covers why that ratio matters.

sections.h2: What a portable heater actually costs per hour
sections.html:
Most portable heaters sold in the UK are rated at 2kW or 3kW. Running flat out, a 2kW heater uses 2 kWh every hour, which costs 52.2p at the current cap. A 3kW model costs just over 78p an hour at full power.

In practice they rarely run flat out for long. Once a small room is up to temperature, a heater with a thermostat cycles on and off, and over an evening it might draw only 40 to 60 per cent of its rated power on average. Call it roughly 1 kWh per hour for a 2kW heater holding a normal-sized room warm, or about 26p an hour. In a large, draughty or poorly insulated room it can genuinely run continuously, and then you really do pay the full 52p an hour.

One myth to clear up early: the type of heater barely matters. Fan, convector, oil-filled, halogen, ceramic and panel heaters all cost exactly the same per kWh, because they are all just resistance elements. They differ in how the heat feels and how fast it arrives, not in what it costs. The full breakdown by type is in our guide to portable heater running costs.

sections.h2: What gas central heating costs per hour
sections.html:
A central heating boiler is rated at 24kW to 35kW, but that is its maximum output, not what it burns each hour. Once the house is warm, a modern boiler modulates down and cycles, and a typical three-bedroom house draws something like 4 to 6 kWh of gas per hour of heating on an average winter day, rising to 8 kWh or more in a cold snap or in a big, leaky house.

At 7.33p per kWh that works out at roughly 30p to 45p an hour in normal winter weather, and perhaps 60p to 75p when it is properly cold. The striking comparison is this: heating your entire house with gas often costs about the same per hour as running a single 2kW heater flat out in one room. We work through the figures in more detail, including how house size and insulation shift them, in what central heating costs per hour.

One thing neither option avoids is the standing charge. You pay the 29.04p daily gas standing charge whether the boiler fires or not, so leaving the heating off and plugging in an electric heater does not save you that fixed cost unless you disconnect the gas supply entirely.

sections.h2: When the portable heater genuinely wins
sections.html:
The portable heater earns its keep in exactly one scenario: it replaces the central heating rather than adding to it, and it heats a small fraction of the house for a limited time. Think of one person working from home in a box room, or someone living mostly in one room of a larger house.

Here is a typical five-hour evening, costed both ways:

Scenario (5 hours)Energy usedCost
2kW heater, one average room, thermostat cycling5 kWh electricity£1.31
2kW heater running flat out in a draughty room10 kWh electricity£2.61
Gas central heating, 3-bed house, mild winter day25 kWh gas£1.83
Gas central heating, 3-bed house, cold snap40 kWh gas£2.93
Two 2kW heaters in two rooms, cycling10 kWh electricity£2.61

At 26.11p per kWh electricity and 7.33p per kWh gas, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026.

Read that table carefully and the pattern jumps out. One heater in one well-behaved room beats whole-house gas by a modest margin, perhaps 50p to £1.50 a night. But the advantage is fragile. Add a second heater, or put the first one in a cold draughty room, and you are already level with or worse than heating the whole house with gas. The rough rule: a portable heater only saves money if it delivers less than about a third of the heat your central heating would have produced over the same period.

The savings are bigger if your alternative is heating a large house for one occupant, and smaller in a compact, well-insulated home where the boiler sips gas anyway. There is a fuller treatment of the trade-off in whether to heat one room or the whole house.

sections.h2: When central heating wins, and the damp trap to avoid
sections.html:
Central heating wins whenever more than one room needs to be warm, whenever heating runs for most of the day, and almost always when the household includes more than one or two people. At three times the price per unit of heat, electricity simply cannot compete once the area being heated grows.

There are two further problems with the heater-only approach that the per-hour sums hide:

  • Condensation and mould

    A house heated in one room stays cold everywhere else, and cold rooms collect condensation. Moisture from cooking, showers and breathing migrates to the coldest surfaces and condenses there, and over a winter that can mean mould on external walls, behind furniture and around windows. Treating a mould problem can cost far more than the heating you saved. If you run this strategy, keep some background heat in the rest of the house and ventilate it.

  • System health

    Boilers, pumps and pipework prefer regular use, and in a hard frost an unheated house risks frozen pipes. Most boilers have frost protection, but only if they are left switched on at the wall.

A smarter middle path usually beats both extremes: run the central heating, but turn radiator valves down or off in rooms you rarely use, drop the room thermostat a degree, and time the heating around your actual day. Our guide to thermostat settings covers how much each degree is worth.

Gas central heating produces heat at roughly 8p per kWh; a plug-in heater produces it at 26.11p per kWh. So central heating is the cheaper way to heat a home, full stop. A portable heater is the cheaper way to heat a person, provided it is one heater, one small room, a few hours, and the boiler stays off the rest of the time.

If that describes your life, the saving is real but modest: typically £15 to £40 a month in winter compared with heating the whole house, and the cheap heater pays for itself in weeks. If it does not, zone your central heating properly instead, set sensible temperatures and timings, and put the boiler on the lowest flow temperature that keeps you comfortable. For the rest of the picture, from insulation to radiator balancing, start with our heating guides.

faq.q: Are oil-filled radiators cheaper to run than fan heaters?
faq.a: No. Every plug-in electric heater converts electricity to heat at the same 100 per cent efficiency, so a kWh of warmth costs 26.11p whichever type you buy. Oil-filled radiators feel gentler and stay warm after switching off, while fan heaters heat a room faster, but over an evening holding the same room at the same temperature they cost the same. The only feature that saves money is an accurate thermostat or timer, because it stops the heater running longer than needed.

faq.q: Is it cheaper to use a portable heater than to run central heating for just one radiator?
faq.a: Usually not. If you turn every other radiator valve down or off, the boiler only feeds the one radiator and you pay roughly 8p per kWh for that heat, against 26.11p per kWh from a plug-in heater. The gas option carries some pipework losses and the boiler cycles less efficiently at very low loads, but it still normally beats electric resistance heating. The portable heater mainly wins on convenience in rooms with no radiator at all.

faq.q: How much does it cost to run a 2kW heater for a day?
faq.a: Flat out, a 2kW heater costs 52.2p an hour at the July to September 2026 cap rate of 26.11p per kWh, so eight hours costs about £4.18 and a full 24 hours about £12.53. With a thermostat cycling it in a normal room, expect roughly half that. Either way, running one all day usually costs more than gas central heating for a whole small house, which is why heater-only heating suits short sessions rather than all-day use.

faq.q: Are infrared or halogen heaters cheaper than convector heaters?
faq.a: Per unit of electricity, no, they all cost 26.11p per kWh at the current cap. Radiant heaters can feel warmer at a lower power setting because they heat you directly rather than the air, so in a large, hard-to-heat space like a garage or conservatory you may be comfortable using fewer kWh overall. In an ordinary insulated living room the practical difference is small.

faq.q: Does heating only one room cause damp in the rest of the house?
faq.a: It can. Household moisture condenses on the coldest surfaces available, and if the rest of the house is left unheated through winter, external walls, window reveals and unused bedrooms become prime spots for condensation and mould. If you rely on a single heated room, ventilate the cold rooms regularly, keep furniture off external walls, and consider brief background heating or a dehumidifier to keep moisture in check.
